msgs.text: Is this a correct mathematical proof of the presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 2-adic numbers as a profinite group?

No. There is a concrete contradiction in the load-bearing **Lemma 3.4** on page 5. The lemma asserts that the standard Demushkin relation can be obtained while also imposing the full unramified marking
\[
\nu_{\rm ur}(\sigma)=1,\qquad \nu_{\rm ur}(x_0)=\nu_{\rm ur}(x_1)=0.
\]


For the relation
\[
x_0^2x_1^4[x_1,\sigma]=1,
\]
Labute’s calculation of the unique Demushkin orientation gives, in these variables,
\[
\chi(x_0)=-1,\qquad \chi(x_1)=1,\qquad
\chi(\sigma)=(1-4)^{-1}.
\]
For the maximal pro-\(2\) Galois group of a local field, Labute identifies this orientation with the cyclotomic character, up to the harmless inverse convention. 

But the abelianization of the displayed presentation is
\[
D^{\rm ab}\cong
\frac{\mathbf Z_2\bar\sigma\oplus\mathbf Z_2\bar x_0
      \oplus\mathbf Z_2\bar x_1}
     {\langle 2\bar x_0+4\bar x_1\rangle}
\cong \mathbf Z_2^2\oplus C_2.
\]
In particular, \(\bar x_1\) has infinite order. Yet Lemma 3.4 would put \(x_1\) simultaneously in
\[
\ker(\nu_{\rm ur})\cap\ker(\chi),
\]
because \(\nu_{\rm ur}(x_1)=0\) and \(\chi(x_1)=1\).

That is impossible for \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}(2)\). Local Kronecker–Weber and local class field theory give
\[
G_{\mathbf Q_2}(2)^{\rm ab}
   \cong \mathbf Z_2\times\mathbf Z_2^\times,
\]
where the two coordinates are precisely the maximal unramified pro-\(2\) character and the \(2\)-adic cyclotomic character. Thus
\[
(\nu_{\rm ur},\chi):
G_{\mathbf Q_2}(2)^{\rm ab}\longrightarrow
\mathbf Z_2\times\mathbf Z_2^\times
\]
is injective. 

Consequently, the assertion in Lemma 3.4 that Labute’s normal-form corrections may all be chosen inside \(\ker\nu_F\) is false. The ordinary **unmarked** Demushkin presentation is valid, but it cannot have the full unramified marking claimed here.

This is fatal to the submitted proof: Proposition 3.5, the boundary fibre product in Section 4, and the terminal induction in Sections 9.1–9.3 explicitly rely on that full \(\mathbf Z_2\)-marking. Therefore the equality of finite-quotient counts and the final reconstruction argument are not established. The proposed abstract presentation might conceivably require a different argument, but this manuscript is not a correct proof of it.
Please tell us how to address this issue and fix the manuscript to be correct and complete.

This cannot be repaired by changing Lemma 3.4 alone. The current relator has the wrong tame/pro-\(2\) overlap, so the statement of Theorem 1.1 must also be changed. Lemma 3.4, Proposition 3.5, the boundary fibre product, and the terminal argument all use the incompatible marking.   

## 1. Replace the marked Demushkin normal form

Write Labute’s standard generators as \(a,b,c\):
\[
D_{\mathrm{std}}
=
\left\langle a,b,c\ \middle|\ a^2b^4[b,c]=1\right\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2}.
\]
With Labute’s orientation convention,
\[
\chi(a)=-1,\qquad \chi(b)=1,\qquad
\chi(c)=(1-4)^{-1}=-\frac13.
\]
Labute’s calculation also shows that the two exceptional directions in the odd-rank correction map are the \(b\)- and \(c\)-directions. 

The correct unramified marking in these variables is
\[
\nu(a)=-2,\qquad \nu(b)=1,\qquad \nu(c)=0,
\]
not \(\nu(c)=1,\nu(a)=\nu(b)=0\). Indeed,
\[
D_{\mathrm{std}}^{\mathrm{ab}}
 \cong
 \mathbf Z_2\bar b\oplus\mathbf Z_2\bar c
 \oplus C_2(\bar a+2\bar b),
\]
and \(\ker\chi\) on the abelianization is precisely the free line
\(\mathbf Z_2\bar b\). Local reciprocity identifies the pro-\(2\) abelianization with
\[
\mathbf Z_2\times\mathbf Z_2^\times
\]
through the unramified and cyclotomic characters; hence \(\nu\) must be nonzero—and may be normalized to \(1\)—on \(\bar b\). 

Introduce relative generators
\[
\varphi=b,\qquad \epsilon=ab^2,\qquad u=c.
\]
Then \(a=\epsilon\varphi^{-2}\), and an exact calculation gives
\[
a^2b^4[b,c]
  =\epsilon\,\epsilon^{\varphi^2}[\varphi,u].
\]
Equivalently, after replacing \(\epsilon\) by its inverse and inverting and cyclically conjugating the relator, one obtains
\[
\epsilon^{\varphi^2}\epsilon[u,\varphi]=1.
\]

The replacement for Lemma 3.4 should therefore be:

> **Lemma 3.4 (unramified-marked dyadic Demushkin normal form).**  
> There are topological generators \(\sigma,x_0,x_1\) of
> \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}(2)\) such that
> \[
> \nu_{\rm ur}(\sigma)=1,\qquad
> \nu_{\rm ur}(x_0)=\nu_{\rm ur}(x_1)=0,
> \]
> \[
> \chi(\sigma)=1,\qquad
> \chi(x_0)=-1,\qquad
> \chi(x_1)=(1-4)^{-1},
> \]
> and the unique defining relation is
> \[
> \boxed{x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0[x_1,\sigma]=1.}
> \]

To make its proof complete, run Labute’s successive approximation in the standard variables with
\[
\nu_F(a)=-2,\quad \nu_F(b)=1,\quad \nu_F(c)=0.
\]
The \(b\)-cokernel direction is detected by \(\nu_F\), while the \(c\)-direction is detected by the principal part of the orientation. Thus a discrepancy annihilated by both characters lies in the image of the correction map restricted to transformations preserving both characters. This is precisely where the current proof goes wrong: it assigns both detecting invariants to the \(c\)-direction and leaves the \(b\)-direction uncontrolled.

## 2. Replace the full profinite relator

Simply replacing \(x_0^2x_1^4\) by \(x_0^{\sigma_2^2}x_0\) is insufficient: it introduces the first derivative \(1+U^{-2}\) in the \(x_0\)-column and invalidates the Jacobian.

There is, however, a concrete corrected block that has the required pro-\(2\) specialization while retaining the first- and second-order coefficient calculations used later. Put
\[
g_0=\sigma_2^2
\]
and define
\[
\boxed{
h_0=
x_0^{g_0}x_0\,
d_0^{g_0}d_0\,
d_0^2\,
[d_0^{g_0},d_0].
}
\]
Replace the present wild relation by
\[
\boxed{
h_0\,u_1^{-1}x_1^\sigma c_0=1.
}
\]

This word has the two necessary specializations:

- In the tame quotient, \(x_0=x_1=u_0=u_1=d_0=c_0=1\), so the new relation disappears.
- In a pro-\(2\) quotient, \(\tau=1\), \(u_i=x_i\), \(d_0=c_0=1\), and \(\sigma_2=\sigma\), so it becomes
  \[
  x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0x_1^{-1}x_1^\sigma
   =x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0[x_1,\sigma]=1.
  \]

It also preserves the coefficient calculations. If the lift variables on
\((\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1)\) are \((a,b,c,d)\), let
\[
P=1+T+\cdots+T^{e-1},\qquad U=S^{\omega_2}.
\]
Then
\[
D d_0=P b+(P+1)c.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\begin{aligned}
D h_0
 &=(1+U^{-2})c
 +(1+U^{-2})\bigl(Pb+(P+1)c\bigr)\\
 &=P(1+U^{-2})(b+c).
\end{aligned}
\]
On a simple tame module this is zero: either \(P=0\), or \(P=1\), in which case \(T=1\) and the \(2\)-primary part \(U\) acts trivially.

The same block has exactly the old second-order contribution. For a normalized class supported on \(x_0\), with values \(c\) and \(\lambda\) in the two Heisenberg coordinates:

- when \(P=1\), one has \(U=1\) and \(Dd_0=0\), so \(h_0\) contributes \(\lambda(c)\);
- when \(P=0\), put \(A=U^{-2}\), \(e=\lambda(c)\),
  \(p=(A\lambda)(c)\), and \(q=\lambda(Ac)\). The first four factors of \(h_0\) contribute \(p+q\), the square \(d_0^2\) contributes \(e\), and the commutator contributes \(p+q\). Their sum is \(e=\lambda(c)\).

The corresponding extraspecial calculation is
\[
B(Ac,c)+q(c)+B(Ac,c)=q(c).
\]
Thus \(h_0\) has the same mixed and quadratic coefficient as the former \(x_0^2x_1^4\) block: \(x_0^2\) supplied that coefficient, while \(x_1^4\) had zero first and second coefficient jet.

## 3. Required manuscript edits

The revised main theorem should use \(h_0u_1^{-1}x_1^\sigma c_0\), and Proposition 3.3 should become
\[
\Pi_{\rm ur}=
\left\langle \sigma,x_0,x_1\ \middle|\
x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0[x_1,\sigma]=1
\right\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2}.
\]
To connect this with Labute’s presentation, set
\[
a=x_0^{-1}\sigma^{-2},\qquad b=\sigma,\qquad c=x_1.
\]
Then
\[
a^2b^4[b,c]
=x_0^{-1}(x_0^{-1})^{\sigma^2}[\sigma,x_1],
\]
whose inverse is cyclically conjugate to the new relator.

In Sections 5 and 6:

- Replace the rows for \(x_0^2\) and \(x_1^4\) by one row for \(h_0\).
- Add the replacement-word calculation above.
- The displayed Jacobian (16), mixed pairing (29), quadratic formula (33), polar form (35), and subsequent Arf calculations may then remain unchanged.
- Replace phrases such as “the \(x_0^2\)-contribution” by “the \(h_0\)-contribution.”

The boundary
\[
T_{\rm tame}\times_{\mathbf Z_2}\Pi_{\rm ur}
\]
should remain a fibre product over the full \(\mathbf Z_2\), not merely over \(C_2\). The terminal argument explicitly needs reductions modulo every \(2^m\). 

Finally, update Appendix A and the machine-readable block, rerun every finite computation with the new word, and remove the present “computationally verified” claim until those computations are reproducible. A useful mandatory regression test is that
\[
(\nu_{\rm ur},\chi):
\Pi_{\rm ur}^{\rm ab}\longrightarrow
\mathbf Z_2\times\mathbf Z_2^\times
\]
is an isomorphism. The old word fails this test; the corrected relative quotient passes it.

This repairs the identified fatal incompatibility and gives a coherent way to retain most of the lifting and induction architecture. It does not by itself certify unrelated arguments in the 25-page manuscript, so the local determinant and global induction sections should still receive an independent line-by-line audit before the revised theorem is claimed.

The 2-adic Absolute Galois Group

About the problem

The absolute Galois group of a field  is the projective limit of all of the finite Galois groups . It packages together the information about all finite extensions, and studying the Galois group of the rational field  is a central problem in algebraic number theory. One method for approaching this group is to study the analogous Galois groups of p-adic fields . In this case, there is an explicit presentation of  for  . For  , we have a description for the absolute Galois group of some extensions of , but not for  itself. Finding such a description would fill in a gap in our explicit understanding of Galois groups, and would have consequences in terms of counting p-adic fields with a given Galois group.



Warm-up: We ask for a presentation of the absolute Galois group of , which is known.

AI prompts

Warm-up

Find a presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 3-adic numbers.  It may be helpful to know that the maximal pro-3 quotient of the absolute Galois group is a free group on 2 generators, and that for every finite tamely ramified extension $K/\mathbb{Q}_3$ the maximal pro-3 quotient of $\Gal(\overline{K}/K)$ is a Demuskin group.



You should give your output as two or three sections, separated by blank lines: variables, definitions (optional) and relations.



The variables section should consist of a comma separated list of variable names on one line corresponding to the generators of the presentation.  The first and second generators (say $\sigma$ and $\tau$ respectively) are distinguished, and must satisfy the implicit relation $\tau^\sigma = \tau^3$ (which should not be included in the \texttt{relations} section).  All later generators should be contained within the $3$-core of the group (the intersection of all $3$-Sylow subgroups).



In the definitions section, you may define additional variables in terms of the generators and variables defined earlier within the definitions section.  Each line should take the form "var = expr", where expr is an expression built using group operations.



In the relations section, you should give the relations as expressions in terms of variables defined in the previous two sections.  If there are multiple relations, give one on each line.



Expressions in profinite groups may involve exponents lying in $\hat{\Z}$.  To specify such a constant, you should provide its reduction modulo $5354228880 = 2^4 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5 \cdot 7 \cdot 11 \cdot 13 \cdot 17 \cdot 19 \cdot 23$.



Example format for the solution (this is a presentation for the maximal pro-2 quotient of the 2-adic absolute Galois group



z,tau,x,y



x^2*y^3*y^z

Full problem

Find a presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 2-adic numbers.  It may be helpful to know that the maximal pro-2 quotient of the absolute Galois group can be presented with three generators x,y,z and one relation, x^2*y^3*y^z=1.  The theory of Demuskin groups may also be useful.



You should give your output as two or three sections, separated by blank lines: variables, definitions (optional) and relations.



The variables section should consist of a comma separated list of variable names on one line corresponding to the generators of the presentation.  The first and second generators (say $\sigma$ and $\tau$ respectively) are distinguished, and must satisfy the implicit relation $\tau^\sigma = \tau^2$ (which should not be included in the \texttt{relations} section).  All later generators should be contained within the $2$-core of the group (the intersection of all $2$-Sylow subgroups).



In the definitions section, you may define additional variables in terms of the generators and variables defined earlier within the definitions section.  Each line should take the form "var = expr", where expr is an expression built using group operations.



In the relations section, you should give the relations as expressions in terms of variables defined in the previous two sections.  If there are multiple relations, give one on each line.



Expressions in profinite groups may involve exponents lying in $\hat{\Z}$.  To specify such a constant, you should provide its reduction modulo $85667662080 = 2^8 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5 \cdot 7 \cdot 11 \cdot 13 \cdot 17 \cdot 19 \cdot 23$.



Example format for the solution (this is a presentation for p=3)



sigma,tau,x0,x1



tau2 = tau^3011753745

sigma2 = sigma^3011753745

rho = tau2^4

angle = (x0*tau*x0^-1*tau)^2974571600

u = (x1*(rho^2)*x1*(rho^2))^2974571600

v = sigma2*tau2

w = (u*(v^2)*u^-1*(v^2))^2974571600

y1 = x1^rho*(u^v)*(w^(sigma2*tau2^2))*(w^(tau2^2))

comm = x1^-1*y1^-1*x1*y1



(x0^sigma)^-1*angle*x1^3*comm

How should we adjust the candidate and or/the proof?

**Both must be fixed.** In the marked format required by the Epoch problem, the present candidate is incompatible with \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}\), and changing the relator forces the candidate-side proof to be redone. Fixing only Lemma 3.4 will not suffice.

I should also qualify my previous message: the proposed \(h_0\)-word was an engineered **trial candidate**, not a proved correction.

## Why the present candidate must change

The manuscript’s wild relation specializes at \(\tau=1\) to
\[
x_0^2x_1^4[x_1,\sigma]=1.
\]
It then claims that \(\sigma\) is fully unramified while \(x_0,x_1\) lie in the kernel of the unramified character.  

This is not merely a bad choice of proof coordinates. Labute’s intrinsic Demushkin orientation for
\[
\langle a,b,c\mid a^2b^4[b,c]=1\rangle
\]
satisfies
\[
\chi(a)=-1,\qquad \chi(b)=1,\qquad
\chi(c)=(1-4)^{-1}=-\frac13.
\]
These values are part of the intrinsic dualizing orientation of the Demushkin group. 

In the manuscript’s variables, \(a=x_0,b=x_1,c=\sigma\). Its abelianization is therefore
\[
D_{\mathrm{old}}^{\mathrm{ab}}
 =
 \frac{\mathbf Z_2\bar\sigma\oplus
       \mathbf Z_2\bar x_0\oplus
       \mathbf Z_2\bar x_1}
      {\langle2\bar x_0+4\bar x_1\rangle}.
\]
The class \(\bar x_1\) has infinite order, while the proposed marking gives
\[
\nu_{\rm ur}(x_1)=0,\qquad \chi(x_1)=1.
\]

For \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}(2)\), local reciprocity identifies the maximal pro-\(2\) abelian quotient with
\[
\widehat{\mathbf Q_2^\times}^{(2)}
 \cong \mathbf Z_2\times\mathbf Z_2^\times,
\]
where the factors are the unramified and cyclotomic coordinates, up to the usual reciprocity inversion convention. Thus \((\nu_{\rm ur},\chi)\) is injective on the abelianization. Local class field theory identifies units with abelian inertia and a uniformizer with Frobenius. 

Moreover, the normal pro-\(2\) subgroup generated by \(x_0,x_1\) is intrinsic here: the full tame quotient has no nontrivial normal pro-\(2\) subgroup, so this subgroup is the \(2\)-core. Any isomorphism with \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}\) would therefore send it to wild inertia and preserve the kernel of the unramified character. Consequently, the incompatibility cannot be removed by an unmarked abstract isomorphism.

So the present wild relator should not remain the theorem’s candidate.

## The correct marked maximal pro-\(2\) boundary

The firm correction is the following relative Demushkin presentation:
\[
\boxed{
G_{\mathbf Q_2}(2)
 \cong
 \left\langle\sigma,x_0,x_1\ \middle|\
 x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0[x_1,\sigma]=1
 \right\rangle_{\mathrm{pro}-2}
}
\]
with
\[
\nu_{\rm ur}(\sigma)=1,\qquad
\nu_{\rm ur}(x_0)=\nu_{\rm ur}(x_1)=0,
\]
and
\[
\chi(\sigma)=1,\qquad
\chi(x_0)=-1,\qquad
\chi(x_1)=-\frac13.
\]

It is obtained from Labute’s standard variables by
\[
\sigma=b,\qquad
x_0=(ab^2)^{-1},\qquad
x_1=c.
\]
Indeed \(a=x_0^{-1}\sigma^{-2}\), and
\[
a^2b^4[b,c]
 =
 x_0^{-1}(x_0^{\sigma^2})^{-1}
 [x_1,\sigma]^{-1}.
\]
After inversion and cyclic conjugation, this is precisely
\[
x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0[x_1,\sigma].
\]

Its abelianization is now correctly aligned with local reciprocity:
\[
D_{\mathrm{rel}}^{\mathrm{ab}}
 \cong
 \mathbf Z_2\bar\sigma
 \oplus\mathbf Z_2\bar x_1
 \oplus C_2\bar x_0.
\]
The three coordinates are respectively unramified, principal cyclotomic, and cyclotomic sign.

The new manuscript should prove this as a genuine **marked** version of the Demushkin normal form, rather than cite the ordinary unmarked classification.

## How to search for the full profinite relator

Keep the definitions
\[
\sigma_2=\sigma^{\omega_2},\qquad
u_i=(x_i\tau)^{\omega_2},\qquad
d_0=u_0x_0^{-1},\qquad
c_0=[d_0,x_0^{\sigma_2}].
\]
Put \(g=\sigma_2^2\).

The simplest corrected starting point is
\[
\boxed{
R_0=x_0^g x_0\,u_1^{-1}x_1^\sigma c_0.
}
\]
It has the two required specializations:

\[
x_0=x_1=1\quad\Longrightarrow\quad R_0=1
\]
in the tame quotient, while
\[
\tau=1\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
R_0=x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0[x_1,\sigma]
\]
in the maximal pro-\(2\) quotient.

Its full abelianization also gives \(2\bar x_0=0\), as required. However, its evaluated Fox Jacobian differs from the one in the current manuscript, so Sections 5–6 cannot simply be copied.

A more elaborate trial word discussed previously is
\[
H_0=
x_0^g x_0\,
d_0^g d_0\,
d_0^2\,
[d_0^g,d_0],
\qquad
R_\ast=H_0u_1^{-1}x_1^\sigma c_0.
\]
This also passes the tame, pro-\(2\), and abelianization tests and is designed to cancel an unwanted first-order term. But it remains an **ansatz**. It must not be advertised as the answer until its universal Fox, Heisenberg, extraspecial, and finite-quotient calculations have been independently verified.

A systematic procedure is preferable: begin with \(R_0\), compute its first and second derivatives, and multiply it by correction words that are trivial under both specialization maps
\[
x_0,x_1\mapsto1
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\tau\mapsto1.
\]
Such corrections preserve both boundary quotients while allowing the module-valued Jacobian and quadratic obstruction to be tuned.

## What survives from the manuscript

| Part | Status |
|---|---|
| Candidate construction and reconstruction in §2 | Reusable after replacing the relator |
| Tame quotient, Lemma 3.1 and Proposition 3.2 | Likely reusable |
| Propositions 3.3–3.5 and Lemma 3.4 | Must be replaced |
| Fibre-product boundary in §4 | Reusable only after the corrected marked pro-\(2\) lemma |
| Candidate Fox–Heisenberg calculations in §§5–6.2 | Must be recomputed from the new exact word |
| Local Tate-duality calculations | Logically independent, but still require expert audit |
| Group-theoretic induction in §§7–10 | Potentially reusable once all its candidate-side hypotheses are re-established |
| Computational appendix | Must be regenerated with reproducible code and test data |

In particular, the terminal argument explicitly uses the complete \(\mathbf Z_2\)-marking, not merely its reduction modulo \(2\). 

## Recommended order of work

1. **Withdraw the current Theorem 1.1 as proved.** Retitle the draft as a candidate or working manuscript.

2. **Write a short standalone proof of the corrected marked Demushkin lemma.** Include the orientation values, local-reciprocity matrix, the change of variables above, and a marking-preserving normal-form argument.

3. **Choose the full wild word only after symbolic testing.** Test \(R_0\) first; use \(R_\ast\) only as an alternative experimental candidate.

4. **Require these regression tests before rebuilding the proof:**
   \[
   R|_{\rm tame}=1,\qquad
   R|_{\rm pro-2}=x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0[x_1,\sigma],
   \]
   \[
   \Gamma^{\rm ab,(2)}
   \cong\mathbf Z_2\oplus\mathbf Z_2\oplus C_2,
   \]
   and the matrix of \((\nu_{\rm ur},\chi)\) on the two free abelian directions must have \(2\)-adic unit determinant.

5. **Recompute the exact candidate-side deformation theory.** This includes the Fox Jacobian, mixed cup pairing, quadratic connecting maps, Arf invariants, and all naturality identities. Matching only the pro-\(2\) quotient is not enough.

6. **Then rerun the finite-quotient induction and computation.** The computational verification must include code, the tested target groups, marked counts, and independent reproducibility.

The current Epoch page still lists the full problem as unsolved.  What has been established here is the necessary corrected maximal pro-\(2\) boundary and a concrete route for searching for the full relator—not yet a complete presentation.
How come these riveting research turns are still drilling on A_2? what are they getting wrong, if anything?

According to a document from June 23, 2026, they are still drilling on \(A_2\) because Turn 4 effectively froze it as the baseline, and every subsequent failure was treated as a defect in the proof machinery rather than as a reason to recheck the candidate’s foundational marking. Turn 129 still says “the candidate remains unchanged” and audits only the active-block and determinant machinery.  

## The foundational error

For every pro-\(2\) quotient of \(A_2\),
\[
\tau=1,\qquad u_i=x_i,\qquad d_0=c_0=1.
\]
Thus its wild relation necessarily specializes to
\[
x_0^2x_1^4[x_1,\sigma]=1.
\]

The manuscript then asserts the **marked** presentation
\[
\nu_{\mathrm{ur}}(\sigma)=1,\qquad
\nu_{\mathrm{ur}}(x_0)=\nu_{\mathrm{ur}}(x_1)=0
\]
for that exact relator. This is Lemma 3.4, and the rest of the boundary construction depends on it.  

But Labute’s intrinsic orientation for
\[
a^2b^4[b,c]=1
\]
is, up to the usual inverse convention,
\[
\chi(a)=-1,\qquad
\chi(b)=1,\qquad
\chi(c)=(1-4)^{-1}.
\]
In the manuscript’s naming, \(a=x_0,\ b=x_1,\ c=\sigma\), so
\[
\chi(x_1)=1.
\]
Labute computes these values directly from the relator. 

Meanwhile,
\[
D^{\mathrm{ab}}
=
\frac{\mathbf Z_2\bar\sigma\oplus
      \mathbf Z_2\bar x_0\oplus
      \mathbf Z_2\bar x_1}
     {\langle2\bar x_0+4\bar x_1\rangle}
\cong \mathbf Z_2^2\oplus C_2,
\]
and \(\bar x_1\) has infinite order.

The claimed marking therefore puts an infinite-order abelian class simultaneously in
\[
\ker\nu_{\mathrm{ur}}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\ker\chi.
\]

That cannot happen for \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}(2)\). Local reciprocity identifies its pro-\(2\) abelianization with the pro-\(2\) completion of
\[
\mathbf Q_2^\times
\cong 2^{\mathbf Z}\times\mathbf Z_2^\times,
\]
and the unramified and cyclotomic coordinates jointly give an injective map
\[
(\nu_{\mathrm{ur}},\chi):
G_{\mathbf Q_2}(2)^{\mathrm{ab}}
\longrightarrow
\mathbf Z_2\times\mathbf Z_2^\times.
\]


So Lemma 3.4 is not merely insufficiently proved: its conclusion is incompatible with local class field theory.

## Why 129 turns did not expose it

They repeatedly conflate two statements:

1. \(A_2\) has the correct maximal pro-\(2\) group **abstractly**.
2. It has the correct maximal pro-\(2\) group with the designated **wild/unramified marking**.

The first is true: the displayed relator is a standard Demushkin relator. The second is false.

That distinction evades most of their tests:

- Pure \(2\)-group targets only see the abstract Demushkin group.
- Mod-\(2\) cup products see the Hilbert form, but not the full lift of the unramified character to \(\mathbf Z_2\).
- Selected groups such as \(\mathrm{GL}_2(3)\) test nonlinear obstruction data, not the full local-reciprocity marking.
- Turn 50 explicitly declares that \(\sigma^{\omega_2}\) is the unramified direction and that \(\langle\!\langle x_0,x_1\rangle\!\rangle\) is wild inertia. That is precisely the claim contradicted above. 
- Turn 52 says the candidate and local group have the same maximal pro-\(2\) presentation “after the generator naming”; this silently upgrades an unmarked isomorphism to a marked one. 

There is also an unresolved mismatch between versions of the proof. Turn 112 uses only
\[
T\times_{C_2}\Pi_{\mathrm{el}},
\]
recording the parity of \(\sigma\). That forgets the higher \(2^m\)-unramified data where the contradiction lives. The manuscript instead uses a fibre product over the full \(\mathbf Z_2\), but that stronger boundary rests on false Lemma 3.4. 

## What the marked pro-\(2\) boundary should look like

In Labute’s standard variables, the unramified direction is the \(b\)-direction, not the \(c\)-direction. A compatible change of variables is
\[
\sigma=b,\qquad
x_0=(ab^2)^{-1},\qquad
x_1=c.
\]
Then
\[
\nu_{\mathrm{ur}}(\sigma)=1,\qquad
\nu_{\mathrm{ur}}(x_0)=\nu_{\mathrm{ur}}(x_1)=0,
\]
and the standard relator becomes, after inversion and cyclic conjugation,
\[
\boxed{x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0[x_1,\sigma]=1.}
\]

This is the necessary relative Demushkin boundary.

Because \(c_0=1\) whenever \(\tau=1\), no reinterpretation of the existing proof can make \(A_2\) specialize to this relation. The full candidate word itself has to change.

## Verdict

They are constructing increasingly sophisticated conditional machinery around a frozen coordinate error. Much of the Fox–Heisenberg, determinant, and finite-target technology may remain useful, but it currently proves nothing about the claimed \(A_2\) presentation because its common marked boundary is wrong.

The correct course is:

1. reject Lemma 3.4 and the current \(A_2\) candidate;
2. impose \(x_0^{\sigma^2}x_0[x_1,\sigma]\) as the required pro-\(2\) specialization;
3. redesign the full mixed relator;
4. recompute all first- and second-order obstruction data;
5. remove the manuscript’s “computationally verified” claim—the latest log still says the private 5402-group verifier has not been run.
